---
title: "First and Last of User Selected Tempurature"
author: "Wendy Horvat"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
runtime: shiny
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```
---

# Interactive Exploration of Temperature Threshold Events in Portland, Oregon

## Overview

This document explores historical temperature threshold events in Portland, Oregon using daily weather observations. Instead of using a fixed definition of freezing conditions, this version allows the user to explore how different **temperature thresholds** affect the frequency and timing of cold-weather events.

The analysis is implemented using an interactive application built with Shiny.

---

# What is Shiny?

The **Shiny** package allows users to build interactive web applications directly in R.

A Shiny app combines:

* A **user interface (UI)** for controls (sliders, buttons, dropdowns)
* A **server function** that processes data
* **Reactive outputs** that automatically update when inputs change

### Why use Shiny here?

In a static report:

* the temperature threshold is fixed (e.g., 32°F)
* plots must be regenerated manually to test new values

In a Shiny app:

* users can adjust the temperature threshold dynamically
* visualizations update instantly without re-running code

This makes the analysis more exploratory and flexible.

---

# Required Packages

```r id="p2k9rt"
library(shiny)
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(ggtext)
```

### Package roles

* **shiny** → builds interactive applications
* **tidyverse** → data manipulation and visualization tools
* **lubridate** → simplifies working with dates
* **ggtext** → allows styled text in plot annotations

---

# Load the Data

```r id="n1q8ab"
csv_data <- read_csv("data/portland_1871_11_2022-12_temp_prec.csv")
```

This dataset contains daily weather observations, including temperature and date information.

---

# Shiny Application Structure

A Shiny application has two main components:

### 1. User Interface (UI)

Defines what the user sees and interacts with.

### 2. Server

Defines how the data responds to user input.

---

# User Interface (UI)

```r id="v8x2jk"
ui <- fluidPage(
  
  titlePanel("Portland Temperature Threshold Explorer"),
  
  sidebarLayout(
    
    sidebarPanel(
      sliderInput(
        inputId = "temp_threshold",
        label = "Temperature threshold (°F)",
        min = 28,
        max = 40,
        value = 32,
        step = 1
      )
    ),
    
    mainPanel(
      plotOutput("tempPlot", height = "700px")
    )
  )
)
```

### UI components explained

* **fluidPage()**
  Creates a responsive web layout.

* **titlePanel()**
  Displays the application title.

* **sidebarLayout()**
  Splits the page into:

  * a control panel (left)
  * a visualization panel (right)

* **sliderInput()**
  Creates an interactive slider:

  * `inputId` identifies the input in server logic
  * `min`, `max`, `step` define the allowed range
  * `value` sets the default threshold

* **plotOutput()**
  Allocates space for the interactive plot.

---

# Server Logic

```r id="k3m9cz"
server <- function(input, output, session) {
  
  output$tempPlot <- renderPlot({
    
    TEMP_THRESHOLD <- input$temp_threshold
```

### Reactivity concept

* `input$temp_threshold` updates automatically when the slider moves
* `renderPlot()` ensures the plot is recalculated whenever inputs change

---

## Prepare the data

```r id="q9t3lm"
extract_dates <- csv_data %>%
  mutate(
    year = year(DATE),
    month = month(DATE),
    yday = yday(DATE)
  )
```

This creates:

| Variable | Meaning                         |
| -------- | ------------------------------- |
| year     | Calendar year                   |
| month    | Month of observation            |
| yday     | Day of year (seasonal position) |

---

## Identify all threshold events

```r id="r6w1zn"
threshold_days <- extract_dates %>%
  filter(TMIN <= TEMP_THRESHOLD)
```

These are all days where the minimum temperature is at or below the selected threshold.

---

## First threshold event (fall/winter)

```r id="t4x8qp"
first_threshold <- extract_dates %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  filter(TMIN <= TEMP_THRESHOLD, month > 7) %>%
  summarise(min = min(DATE), .groups = "drop")
```

* Restricts search to August–December
* Identifies the first cold event of the cool season

---

## Last threshold event (winter/spring)

```r id="m7v2sd"
last_threshold <- extract_dates %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  filter(TMIN <= TEMP_THRESHOLD, month < 7) %>%
  summarise(max = max(DATE), .groups = "drop")
```

* Restricts search to January–June
* Identifies the final cold event before warmer months

---

## Convert to seasonal timeline

```r id="z1p5wr"
first_threshold_yday <- first_threshold %>%
  mutate(yday = yday(min))

last_threshold_yday <- last_threshold %>%
  mutate(yday = yday(max))
```

This allows all years to be aligned on a shared seasonal axis.

---

# Interactive Plot

```r id="c7n2ld"
ggplot() +
  
  geom_point(
    data = threshold_days,
    aes(y = year, x = as.Date(yday, origin = "2020-01-01")),
    color = "gray",
    size = 0.8
  ) +
  
  geom_point(
    data = first_threshold_yday,
    aes(y = year, x = as.Date(yday, origin = "2020-01-01")),
    fill = "purple",
    shape = 21,
    size = 3,
    color = "black"
  ) +
  
  geom_point(
    data = last_threshold_yday,
    aes(y = year, x = as.Date(yday, origin = "2020-01-01")),
    fill = "blue",
    shape = 21,
    size = 3,
    color = "black"
  ) +
  
  scale_y_reverse() +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%b", breaks = "month") +
  
  theme_minimal() +
  
  labs(
    title = paste("Temperature threshold:", TEMP_THRESHOLD, "°F")
  )
```

---

### Interpretation of layers

| Layer         | Meaning                               |
| ------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Gray points   | All days at or below threshold        |
| Purple points | First threshold event of fall/winter  |
| Blue points   | Last threshold event of spring/winter |

Each layer is independent and can be modified without affecting others.

---

# Run the Application

```r id="f8q2km"
shinyApp(ui, server)
```

This launches a local interactive application where:

* the slider controls the temperature threshold
* the plot updates instantly
* no manual re-execution is needed

---

# Summary

This Shiny application converts a static climate analysis into an interactive exploration tool. Users can now adjust the temperature threshold dynamically to examine how different definitions of cold-weather events influence seasonal patterns over time.

---

Next to-do revision:

* rename variables so they are more consistent (e.g., `threshold_event_days` everywhere)
